question_text: A car of mass 1000 kg is moving along a circular road of radius 100 m. The centripetal force required to keep the car on the circular path when it travels at 10 m/s is:
explanation: Centripetal force is given by F = mv²/r. Here m = 1000 kg, v = 10 m/s, r = 100 m. F = 1000 × (10)² / 100 = 1000 × 100 / 100 = 1000 N. This force is provided by friction between the tyres and the road on a level road. The centripetal force always acts towards the centre of the circular path and is responsible for the change in direction of velocity without changing its magnitude.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 100 N would result if you forgot to square the velocity or made an arithmetic error. Check: F = mv²/r = 1000 × 100 / 100 = 1000 N, not 100 N.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not squaring the velocity or making an arithmetic error in the division.

elimination_hints.hint: 10000 N would result if the radius were 10 m rather than 100 m. Re-read the problem: r = 100 m, giving F = 1000 N.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using r = 10 m instead of r = 100 m, or multiplying instead of dividing by r.

elimination_hints.hint: 500 N would result if half the mass (500 kg) were used or if r were taken as 200 m. The correct mass is 1000 kg and r = 100 m.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly halving the mass or doubling the radius in the formula.

question_text: On a level circular road, what provides the centripetal force to a vehicle moving in a circle?
explanation: On a level (unbanked) circular road, the only horizontal force acting on the vehicle is the static friction between the tyres and the road surface. This friction acts radially inward (towards the centre of the circle) and provides the necessary centripetal force. The normal reaction from the road is vertical and only balances gravity. Gravity acts downward and has no horizontal component to contribute to centripetal force.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: The normal reaction acts perpendicular to the road surface (vertically upward on a level road). It cannot provide any horizontal centripetal force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the direction of normal reaction; it is vertical, not horizontal, on a level road.

elimination_hints.hint: Gravity acts vertically downward. On a level road it has no horizontal component, so it cannot contribute to centripetal (radially inward) force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking gravity has a horizontal component on a level surface.

elimination_hints.hint: Engine thrust drives the vehicle forward (tangential direction). It cannot provide the inward centripetal force needed for circular motion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing tangential (forward) driving force with the radially inward centripetal force.

question_text: A stone tied to a string is whirled in a vertical circle. The tension in the string is maximum at which position?
explanation: At the bottom of the vertical circle, both tension (upward) and centripetal acceleration point upward (toward centre), while gravity points downward. Newton's second law: T_bottom − mg = mv²_bottom/r, so T_bottom = mg + mv²_bottom/r. At the top, both tension and gravity point downward (toward centre): T_top + mg = mv²_top/r, giving T_top = mv²_top/r − mg. By energy conservation, speed is greatest at the bottom and least at the top. Since mg adds to tension at the bottom (and subtracts at the top), tension is maximum at the bottom of the circle.
subject: physics

options.text: At the top of the circle
At the horizontal level of the centre
At the bottom of the circle

elimination_hints.hint: At the top, both tension and gravity point toward the centre (downward), giving T_top = mv²_top/r − mg. The mg term reduces tension and the speed is lowest here, making tension minimum at the top.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking that the highest point requires the most tension to hold the stone up.

elimination_hints.hint: At the horizontal level (side positions), tension = mv²_side/r. The speed here is intermediate between top and bottom, so tension is neither maximum nor minimum.
elimination_hints.misconception: Choosing a symmetric-looking position without analysing the force equation properly.

elimination_hints.hint: At 45° from the bottom, the weight has a component along the string that partially reduces tension. This intermediate position does not give maximum tension.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly assuming a diagonal position maximises string tension.

question_text: A bob of mass m is suspended by a string of length L and whirled in a horizontal circle forming a conical pendulum. If the string makes an angle θ with the vertical, the time period of revolution is:
explanation: For a conical pendulum, resolving forces: T cosθ = mg (vertical equilibrium) and T sinθ = mω²r where r = L sinθ (centripetal force). Dividing these: tanθ = ω²L sinθ/g, giving ω² = g/(L cosθ). The time period T_period = 2π/ω = 2π√(L cosθ/g). This result shows that for small angles (cosθ ≈ 1), the period approaches 2π√(L/g), similar to a simple pendulum. The period decreases as the angle increases (faster rotation needed to maintain a larger angle).
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 2π√(L/g) is the period of a simple pendulum with small oscillations, where cosθ ≈ 1. For a conical pendulum at angle θ, the effective length is L cosθ, not L.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to account for the angle; treating the conical pendulum as a simple pendulum.

elimination_hints.hint: L sinθ is the radius of the horizontal circle, not the effective length determining the period. Balancing vertical forces gives ω² = g/(L cosθ), so cosθ is needed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Substituting the radius r = L sinθ into the simple-pendulum formula instead of the vertical component L cosθ.

elimination_hints.hint: tanθ = sinθ/cosθ is not the relevant ratio here. The vertical equilibrium T cosθ = mg leads to cosθ in the period formula, not tanθ.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly using tanθ instead of cosθ in the period expression.

question_text: A road is banked at an angle θ for a vehicle moving with speed v. The ideal banking condition (no friction needed) is given by:
explanation: On a banked road with no friction, the only forces are the normal reaction N (perpendicular to the road surface) and gravity mg. Resolving: N cosθ = mg (vertical equilibrium) and N sinθ = mv²/r (centripetal force, horizontal). Dividing these equations: tanθ = v²/(rg). This is the ideal banking condition. At this speed, no friction is needed. If the vehicle goes faster than this ideal speed, friction acts inward; if slower, friction acts outward to prevent slipping.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: sinθ = v²/(rg) would be correct only if the vertical and horizontal force equations were swapped. Dividing the correct equations (N sinθ = mv²/r by N cosθ = mg) gives tanθ, not sinθ.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the vertical and horizontal components of the normal force in the force equations.

elimination_hints.hint: cosθ appears in the vertical equilibrium equation (N cosθ = mg), not as the ratio equal to v²/(rg). Dividing the two force equations gives tanθ = v²/(rg).
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the vertical component equation alone rather than the ratio of horizontal to vertical equations.

elimination_hints.hint: tanθ = v/(rg) lacks a square on v. Centripetal acceleration is v²/r (not v/r), so the correct relation involves v² in the numerator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to square the speed; centripetal acceleration is v²/r, not v/r.

question_text: A vehicle travels over a convex bridge (hump) of radius of curvature R at speed v. The normal reaction from the road on the vehicle at the top of the hump is:
explanation: At the top of a convex bridge (hump), the centripetal direction is downward toward the centre of curvature. Applying Newton's second law toward the centre: mg − N = mv²/R. Therefore N = mg − mv²/R = m(g − v²/R). The normal force is reduced compared to a flat road. When v = √(gR), N = 0 and the vehicle just loses contact. For v > √(gR), the vehicle becomes airborne, so √(gR) is the maximum safe speed over the hump.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: N = mg + mv²/R corresponds to the bottom of a concave bridge or the inside of a loop, where centripetal acceleration points upward and N must exceed mg. At the top of a convex hump, centripetal acceleration is downward, so N is reduced.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the formula for the bottom of a loop to the top of a convex bridge.

elimination_hints.hint: N = mv²/R ignores gravity entirely. The correct equation at the top is mg − N = mv²/R, giving N = m(g − v²/R), not just mv²/R.
elimination_hints.misconception: Setting only the centripetal force equal to N, forgetting that gravity also contributes to the net downward force.

elimination_hints.hint: Dividing by 2 has no physical basis here. Newton's second law gives N = m(g − v²/R) directly without any averaging factor.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly averaging forces or making an unjustified algebraic error in the force balance.

question_text: A banked road has banking angle θ and coefficient of static friction μ between tyre and road. The maximum safe speed for a vehicle on this road (radius r) is:
explanation: On a banked road with friction, the maximum speed condition occurs when friction acts inward (down the slope) to prevent the vehicle from skidding outward. After applying Newton's laws in both the vertical and centripetal directions and solving, the standard result is v_max = √[rg(tanθ + μ)/(1 − μ tanθ)]. Both the banking angle and friction cooperate to allow a higher maximum speed compared to a level road. When μ = 0, this reduces to v_ideal = √(rg tanθ), consistent with the ideal banking formula.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Option A gives the minimum safe speed (v_min) on a banked road, not the maximum. At minimum speed, friction acts up the slope (outward) to prevent inward skidding, leading to the expression (tanθ − μ)/(1 + μ tanθ).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the maximum-speed condition (friction acts inward, down slope) with the minimum-speed condition (friction acts outward, up slope).

elimination_hints.hint: Omitting the denominator (1 − μ tanθ) ignores the effect of friction on the normal force's vertical component. The full derivation yields a denominator term that is essential for the correct result.
elimination_hints.misconception: Partially deriving the formula and dropping the denominator correction from the friction contribution to the vertical equation.

elimination_hints.hint: This option mixes sinθ and cosθ inconsistently with the friction term and misses the (1 − μ tanθ) denominator entirely. The correct formula uses tanθ throughout.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly combining the force components without properly dividing the horizontal centripetal equation by the vertical equilibrium equation.

question_text: A particle undergoes uniform circular motion. Which of the following statements is correct?
explanation: In uniform circular motion, the speed (magnitude of velocity) remains constant, but the direction of velocity continuously changes. Since velocity is a vector, a change in direction constitutes a change in velocity, meaning acceleration is not zero. This centripetal acceleration is always directed toward the centre and is perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. The net centripetal force does no work because it is always perpendicular to the displacement.
subject: physics

options.text: Both speed and velocity remain constant
The net force on the particle is zero
The acceleration is in the direction of velocity
The centripetal acceleration is always perpendicular to the velocity

elimination_hints.hint: Speed (scalar magnitude) is constant in uniform circular motion, but velocity (vector) changes direction continuously. Since direction changes, velocity is not constant.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating speed and velocity as identical quantities, ignoring that velocity is a vector with direction.

elimination_hints.hint: If the net force were zero, the particle would move in a straight line (Newton's first law). A net centripetal force mv²/r directed inward is essential for circular motion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing constant speed with zero acceleration or zero net force.

elimination_hints.hint: Acceleration in the direction of velocity would change the speed (tangential acceleration). In uniform circular motion, speed is constant, so acceleration must be perpendicular to velocity, directed centripetally.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing centripetal (perpendicular) acceleration with tangential acceleration that changes speed.

question_text: An object moves in a horizontal circle of radius r with angular velocity ω. In the non-inertial rotating reference frame co-rotating with the object, the centrifugal pseudo force acting on the object has magnitude and direction:
explanation: In a non-inertial reference frame rotating with angular velocity ω, a pseudo force called the centrifugal force appears. For an object of mass m moving in a circle of radius r, the centrifugal force has magnitude mω²r and is directed radially outward (away from the axis of rotation). In the rotating frame, this outward pseudo force balances the inward centripetal force (the real force), making the object appear stationary. Note: Centrifugal force is NOT a real force; it arises due to the non-inertial nature of the rotating frame.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: mω²r inward describes the centripetal force (real force), not the centrifugal pseudo force. The centrifugal force is the apparent outward push in a rotating frame, opposite to the centripetal direction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the direction of centripetal force (inward) with that of the centrifugal pseudo force (outward).

elimination_hints.hint: The centrifugal force magnitude is mω²r, not mωr. The centripetal acceleration is ω²r (not ωr), so the pseudo force magnitude must include the square of ω.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to square the angular velocity ω when writing the centrifugal force magnitude.

elimination_hints.hint: Centrifugal force acts radially outward, not tangentially. A tangential pseudo force (Coriolis force) arises from the velocity of the object relative to the rotating frame.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the radial centrifugal pseudo force with the tangential Coriolis pseudo force.

question_text: A ball of mass m is rotating in a vertical circle of radius r. The minimum speed at the top of the circle so that the string does not go slack is:
explanation: At the top of a vertical circle, both gravity mg and tension T act downward toward the centre. Newton's second law: T + mg = mv²/r. For the string not to go slack, T ≥ 0. Setting T = 0 for the critical (minimum) condition: mg = mv²_min/r, giving v_min = √(gr). At this critical speed, the centripetal acceleration equals g and the string tension is exactly zero. Any speed lower than √(gr) at the top means the available centripetal force is insufficient and the string goes slack.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: √(2gr) is not the critical condition at the top. Setting T = 0: mg = mv²/r gives v = √(gr), not √(2gr). The factor of 2 does not appear in the top-of-circle critical analysis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including an extra factor of 2 in the critical speed, possibly from confusing the top-of-circle result with another formula.

elimination_hints.hint: √(5gr) is the minimum speed required at the bottom of the circle for the ball to complete the full vertical circle (derived by energy conservation from v_top = √(gr)). The question asks for minimum speed at the top, which is √(gr).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the minimum speed needed at the bottom of the loop with the minimum speed at the top.

elimination_hints.hint: √(gr/2) is less than the critical speed √(gr). At this speed, the centripetal acceleration v²/r = g/2 < g, which is insufficient even with T = 0, so the string would be slack.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly halving the gravitational acceleration in the critical condition.

question_text: A ball of mass m is attached to a string of length L and swung in a vertical circle. If the speed at the bottom is v₀, what is the tension in the string when the ball is at the side (same horizontal level as the centre)?
explanation: Using energy conservation between the bottom and the side position (height gain = L): ½mv²_side = ½mv₀² − mgL, giving v²_side = v₀² − 2gL. At the side position, the centripetal direction is horizontal (toward the centre), and gravity acts vertically downward. Only the tension contributes to centripetal force (gravity is purely tangential here). Newton's second law in the centripetal direction: T = mv²_side/L = m(v₀² − 2gL)/L.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Using height = L/2 instead of L gives v²_side = v₀² − gL, which is incorrect. The side position is at a height L above the bottom (one full radius), not L/2.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly using half the string length as the height gained when moving from bottom to side.

elimination_hints.hint: Adding mg would apply if gravity had a radially inward component at the side position. But at the side, gravity is purely tangential (vertical) and does not contribute to tension (centripetal force) at all.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly adding mg to the centripetal force equation at the side position where gravity is perpendicular to the centripetal direction.

elimination_hints.hint: Using v²_side = v₀² + 2gL would imply the ball gained kinetic energy moving upward, violating energy conservation. Speed decreases going up, so the correct expression is v₀² − 2gL.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using incorrect sign in energy conservation, adding potential energy instead of subtracting it.

question_text: A car is moving on a banked circular road of radius 100 m and banking angle 30°. The coefficient of static friction between the tyres and road is 0.3. Taking g = 10 m/s² and tan 30° = 1/√3 ≈ 0.577, the maximum safe speed of the car is approximately:
explanation: Using v_max = √[rg(tanθ + μ)/(1 − μ tanθ)]. Here r = 100 m, g = 10 m/s², tanθ = 0.577, μ = 0.3. Numerator: tanθ + μ = 0.577 + 0.3 = 0.877. Denominator: 1 − μ tanθ = 1 − 0.3 × 0.577 = 1 − 0.173 = 0.827. Ratio = 0.877/0.827 ≈ 1.061. v_max = √(100 × 10 × 1.061) = √1061 ≈ 32.6 m/s ≈ 33 m/s. For comparison, on a level road v_max = √(μrg) = √300 ≈ 17.3 m/s, showing how banking significantly increases the safe speed.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: 17 m/s corresponds to v_max = √(μrg) for a level (unbanked) road. Here the road is banked at 30°, which significantly increases the maximum safe speed beyond 17 m/s.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the banking angle and using only friction to find maximum speed, as if the road were flat.

elimination_hints.hint: 24 m/s is approximately the ideal banking speed √(rg tanθ) = √(100 × 10 × 0.577) ≈ 24 m/s, which ignores the contribution of friction. The actual maximum speed with friction is higher.
elimination_hints.misconception: Computing only the ideal banking speed (no-friction case) instead of the maximum speed with both banking and friction.

elimination_hints.hint: 28 m/s results from arithmetic errors in computing the ratio (tanθ + μ)/(1 − μ tanθ). The correct numerator is 0.877 and denominator is 0.827, giving a ratio ≈ 1.061, not the value that leads to 28 m/s.
elimination_hints.misconception: Correct formula but computational error in the denominator or the final square root evaluation.

question_text:
Assertion (A): A cyclist leans inward while taking a turn on a level road.
Reasoning (R): By leaning inward, the horizontal component of the normal reaction from the road provides the necessary centripetal force.
explanation: When a cyclist leans inward on a level road, the Assertion is correct — cyclists do lean inward to take turns. However, the Reasoning is incorrect: on a level road, the normal reaction from the road is perpendicular to the road surface (vertically upward) and has no horizontal component. It is the static friction between the tyres and road that acts horizontally inward and provides the centripetal force. The lean adjusts the angle so that the resultant of friction and normal force passes through the centre of gravity, preventing toppling. Hence A is correct but R is incorrect.
subject: physics

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Option A requires both A and R to be correct, with R explaining A. While A is correct, R is wrong: on a level road the normal force is vertical and has no horizontal component. Friction provides centripetal force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting the reasoning without checking whether the normal reaction on a level road has a horizontal component.

elimination_hints.hint: Option B requires both statements to be correct. The reasoning R is factually wrong — normal force on a level road is vertical and cannot provide a horizontal centripetal force component.
elimination_hints.misconception: Partially identifying that A is correct but not recognising that R contains a factual error about force direction.

elimination_hints.hint: Option D requires A to be incorrect. But A is correct — cyclists visibly and necessarily lean inward to maintain balance and achieve the required centripetal force through friction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly concluding that leaning inward does not happen or is not necessary for circular motion on a level road.

question_text:
Assertion (A): The minimum speed required at the bottom of a vertical circular loop to complete the full circle is √(5gR), where R is the radius.
Reasoning (R): At the top of the loop, for the critical condition, the tension (or normal force) is zero and gravity alone provides the centripetal force; using energy conservation from bottom to top then yields v_bottom_min = √(5gR).
explanation: At the top of the vertical circle, the critical condition is T = 0. Then mg = mv²_top/R, giving v_top = √(gR). Using energy conservation from bottom to top (height = 2R): ½mv²_bottom = ½mv²_top + mg(2R). Substituting v²_top = gR: v²_bottom = gR + 4gR = 5gR, so v_bottom = √(5gR). Both A and R are correct, and R is precisely the step-by-step explanation that derives the assertion. R correctly identifies: (1) the critical condition T = 0 at the top, and (2) application of energy conservation from bottom to top to derive the result.
subject: physics

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Option B requires R to not explain A. But R directly derives v_bottom = √(5gR) by applying T = 0 at the top and then using energy conservation — this is precisely the explanation that proves assertion A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking R is correct but unrelated to A, when in fact R is the exact derivation that proves A.

elimination_hints.hint: R is not incorrect. R correctly states the critical condition (T = 0 at top gives v_top = √(gR)) and correctly applies energy conservation (height = 2R) to obtain v_bottom = √(5gR). Both steps are valid.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly identifying an error in R's reasoning, perhaps using the wrong height in energy conservation.

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct, not incorrect. The minimum speed at the bottom for completing a vertical circular loop is indeed √(5gR), a well-established result confirmed by the reasoning in R.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly believing the minimum speed at the bottom is different from √(5gR), perhaps confusing it with the minimum speed at the top √(gR).

question_text:
Match the physical situation in Column I with the correct expression or condition in Column II.

Column I:
(P) Ideal banking angle condition
(Q) Maximum speed on a level circular road
(R) Minimum speed at top of vertical circle (string)
(S) Time period of conical pendulum

Column II:
(1) √(μrg)
(2) tanθ = v²/(rg)
(3) 2π√(L cosθ/g)
(4) √(gr)
explanation: P → 2: The ideal banking condition is obtained by resolving forces on a frictionless banked road — N sinθ provides centripetal force and N cosθ balances gravity — giving tanθ = v²/(rg). Q → 1: On a level road, maximum static friction = μmg provides centripetal force, so v_max = √(μrg). R → 4: At the top of a vertical circle, critical condition T = 0 gives mg = mv²/r, so v_min = √(gr). S → 3: For a conical pendulum of string length L at angle θ from vertical, ω² = g/(L cosθ), so T_period = 2π/ω = 2π√(L cosθ/g). Correct matching: P-2, Q-1, R-4, S-3.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: In option A, P is matched to 1 (√μrg) which is the max speed on a level road (scenario Q), not the banking angle condition. The banking angle condition is tanθ = v²/(rg), which is expression 2.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the ideal banking condition (a trigonometric relation) with the maximum speed formula for a level road.

elimination_hints.hint: In option C, R and S are swapped. The minimum speed at the top of a vertical circle is √(gr) (expression 4) and the conical pendulum period is 2π√(L cosθ/g) (expression 3).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the formulas for minimum speed in vertical circular motion and the time period of a conical pendulum.

elimination_hints.hint: In option D, Q is matched to 4 (√gr, the vertical circle critical speed) instead of 1 (√μrg, the level road maximum speed due to friction). These two √(grX) expressions are distinct and must not be confused.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up √(gr) (critical speed at top of vertical circle) with √(μrg) (max speed on level road due to friction).

question_text:
Match the scenario in Column I with the correct expression for the normal force N in Column II.

Column I:
(P) Vehicle at the bottom of a concave bridge (radius R, speed v)
(Q) Vehicle at the top of a convex bridge (radius R, speed v)
(R) Ball at the top of a vertical circular loop (radius R, speed v)
(S) Ball at the bottom of a vertical circular loop (radius R, speed v)

Column II:
(1) N = m(v²/R − g)
(2) N = m(g + v²/R)
(3) N = m(g − v²/R)
(4) N = m(g + v²/R) [same form as 2, for loop bottom]
explanation: P — Bottom of concave bridge: centripetal direction is upward. N − mg = mv²/R → N = m(g + v²/R). Matches expression 2. Q — Top of convex bridge: centripetal direction is downward. mg − N = mv²/R → N = m(g − v²/R). Matches expression 3. R — Top of vertical loop: both N and mg point downward (centripetally). N + mg = mv²/R → N = m(v²/R − g). Matches expression 1. S — Bottom of vertical loop: N points upward (centripetally), mg downward. N − mg = mv²/R → N = m(g + v²/R). Same form as P (expression 2). Correct matching: P-2, Q-3, R-1, S-2.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Option A maps P (bottom of concave bridge) to expression 3 = m(g − v²/R), which is the formula for the top of a convex bridge. At the bottom of a concave bridge, centripetal acceleration is upward, giving N = m(g + v²/R), not m(g − v²/R).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the normal force expressions for the top and bottom of a bridge, not accounting for the direction of centripetal acceleration.

elimination_hints.hint: Option B maps R (top of vertical loop) to expression 2 = m(g + v²/R), which is the formula for the bottom positions. At the top of a loop, both N and mg point toward the centre, giving N = m(v²/R − g), which is expression 1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the bottom-of-loop formula (where N exceeds mg) to the top of a loop (where N is reduced by mg).

elimination_hints.hint: Option C maps Q (top of convex bridge) to expression 1 = m(v²/R − g). But at the top of a convex bridge, N = m(g − v²/R) = expression 3. Expression 1 applies to the top of a vertical loop where N + mg = mv²/R, giving N = m(v²/R − g).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the top-of-convex-bridge formula with the top-of-vertical-loop formula; the key difference is whether mg appears with a positive or negative sign relative to N.

question_text: Centripetal force is a real force that always acts toward the centre of the circular path and is responsible for changing the direction of velocity of an object in uniform circular motion.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Centripetal force is indeed a real force (or the resultant of real forces such as gravity, tension, friction, or normal force component) that acts toward the centre of the circular path. It causes centripetal acceleration a_c = v²/r directed inward. This force continuously changes the direction of the velocity vector without changing its magnitude (in uniform circular motion), keeping the object on the circular path. It is distinct from the centrifugal force, which is a pseudo force in a rotating (non-inertial) frame.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Choosing False would imply centripetal force is not a real force or does not act inward. While centrifugal force is a pseudo force (outward in a rotating frame), centripetal force is real and acts inward toward the centre.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing centripetal force (real, inward) with centrifugal force (pseudo, outward in rotating frame).

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). The statement is clearly either true or false, and the analysis of forces in circular motion confirms it is true.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not applicable.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). The correct answer is A (True), supported by Newton's second law applied to circular motion: F_net = mv²/r directed inward.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not applicable.

question_text: On a banked road, a vehicle always requires friction to move in a circular path regardless of its speed.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. On a banked road, there exists an ideal speed v_ideal = √(rg tanθ) at which the vehicle can navigate the curve without any friction at all. At this speed, the horizontal component of the normal force (N sinθ) exactly provides the required centripetal force, and the vertical component (N cosθ) exactly balances gravity. Friction is needed only when the vehicle moves faster than v_ideal (friction acts inward to prevent outward skidding) or slower than v_ideal (friction acts outward to prevent inward sliding). At exactly the ideal speed, friction force is zero.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: Choosing True would mean friction is always needed on a banked road. But the ideal banking condition tanθ = v²/(rg) is specifically designed so that at one particular speed, no friction is required. The purpose of banking is precisely to reduce friction dependence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking that friction is always needed for circular motion, unaware that banking geometry can provide centripetal force through the normal force component alone at the ideal speed.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). The statement has a clear factual answer: it is False, because at the ideal banking speed friction is zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not applicable.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). The correct answer is B (False), as a vehicle travelling at v = √(rg tanθ) on a banked road requires zero friction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not applicable.

question_text: A particle of mass m moves in a horizontal circle of radius r with uniform speed v. The centripetal acceleration of the particle is ________, and the centripetal force required is ________. If the radius is doubled while keeping the speed constant, the centripetal force becomes ________ of the original value.
explanation: Centripetal acceleration: a_c = v²/r, directed radially inward. Centripetal force: F_c = ma_c = mv²/r, provided by a real inward force. If radius is doubled (r → 2r) with constant v: F_new = mv²/(2r) = (1/2)(mv²/r) = F_c/2. So the centripetal force becomes half (1/2) of the original. This inverse proportionality (F ∝ 1/r at constant speed) explains why larger radius curves require less centripetal force — gentle highway curves need less friction from tyres than sharp curves.
subject: physics

elimination_hints.hint: The centripetal force is mv²/r, not mv/r. You must square the velocity in both the acceleration and force expressions. Also, doubling r gives F_new = mv²/(2r) = half, not one-fourth (one-fourth would result from doubling r twice, i.e. quadrupling r).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not squaring velocity in the centripetal force formula and confusing the effect of doubling versus quadrupling the radius.

elimination_hints.hint: Centripetal acceleration is v²/r, not v/r. The formula a_c = v²/r has dimensions of m/s², requiring v² in the numerator. The force expression mv²/r in this option is correct, but the acceleration expression is wrong.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to square velocity in the centripetal acceleration formula while correctly writing the force formula.

elimination_hints.hint: Doubling r gives F_new = mv²/(2r), which is half the original force, not double. Force and radius are inversely proportional at constant speed (F ∝ 1/r), so increasing r decreases F.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing direct and inverse proportionality: F ∝ 1/r (not F ∝ r) when speed is constant.

question_text:
Scenario: Rohit is designing a mountain highway curve of radius 200 m with a design speed of 20 m/s (g = 10 m/s²). The engineer banks the road at the ideal angle. A vehicle later travels at twice the design speed (40 m/s) on this banked curve.

(i) What is the ideal banking angle θ?
(ii) At 40 m/s, compared to the centripetal force the banking alone provides, the vehicle needs ________ times more centripetal force, so the vehicle tends to:

Select the answer that correctly addresses both (i) and (ii).
explanation: Part (i): Ideal banking: tanθ = v²/(rg) = (20)²/(200 × 10) = 400/2000 = 0.2. So θ = arctan(0.2) ≈ 11.3°. Part (ii): Centripetal force needed at 40 m/s: F_needed = mv²/r = m(40)²/200 = 8m N. Centripetal force provided by banking at design speed: F_banking = mv_design²/r = m(20)²/200 = 2m N. The vehicle now needs 4 times the force that banking alone provides. The extra required centripetal force must come from friction directed inward (down the slope of the bank). If friction is insufficient, the vehicle skids outward (up the banked slope), away from the centre of the curve.
subject: physics

options.text: θ ≈ 5.7° ; vehicle remains stable at all speeds due to banking
θ ≈ 11.3° ; vehicle tends to skid inward (down the banked slope)
θ ≈ 11.3° ; vehicle remains stable at all speeds since the road is banked
θ ≈ 11.3° ; vehicle tends to skid outward (up the banked slope)

elimination_hints.hint: tanθ = v²/(rg) = 400/2000 = 0.2 gives θ ≈ 11.3°, not 5.7°. θ ≈ 5.7° would require tanθ ≈ 0.1, corresponding to a design speed of about 14 m/s, not 20 m/s.
elimination_hints.misconception: Halving the correct tangent value, possibly from computing v/(rg) instead of v²/(rg).

elimination_hints.hint: Inward skidding (down the slope) occurs when the vehicle moves slower than the ideal speed — banking provides more centripetal push than needed. At 40 m/s (greater than 20 m/s), the required centripetal force far exceeds what banking provides, so the vehicle skids outward.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the direction of skidding at speeds above the ideal speed (outward skid) with the direction at speeds below the ideal speed (inward skid).

elimination_hints.hint: Banking provides adequate centripetal force only at the ideal design speed. At twice the design speed, the required centripetal force is four times the ideal (F ∝ v²), far exceeding what banking provides. Without sufficient friction, skidding outward occurs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking that banking provides stability at all speeds, not understanding that ideal banking is designed for only one specific speed.
